original_abstract: Politics and political conflict often occur in the written and spoken word. Scholars have long recognized this, but the massive costs of analyzing even moderately sized collections of texts have hindered their use in political science research. Here lies the promise of automated text analysis: it substantially reduces the costs of analyzing large collections of text. We provide a guide to this exciting new area of research and show how, in many instances, the methods have already obtained part of their promise. But there are pitfalls to using automated methods—they are no substitute for careful thought and close reading and require extensive and problem-specific validation. We survey a wide range of new methods, provide guidance on how to validate the output of the models, and clarify misconceptions and errors in the literature. To conclude, we argue that for automated text methods to become a standard tool for political scientists, methodologists must contribute new methods and new methods of validation.
humanized_abstract: Politics and political conflict are, in large part, conducted in the currency of words: spoken and written. This is a truism that political scientists have long accepted. Yet the cost of conducting the analysis that politics scientists might wish to do has been prohibitively expensive even for relatively modest corpora of text. Automated text analysis tools promise to greatly reduce this cost, delivering most of the promised benefit. We offer a guide to the new world of political science that these new methods open up, highlighting both their power and their pitfalls. While the tools are innovative and seem to promise great gains, it is essential to remember that these methods are no substitute for hard thinking or close attention to a text and will therefore need to be heavily validated for specific use cases. We summarize many of the approaches and their validity and attempt to correct several errors and misconceptions in the literature and plead for more methods and methodologies of validation in order for the field to develop fully and that the promised methods and tools of the field of political science become a part of the standard arsenal of the political scientist.
